messages.content:
You are a GDPR compliance analyst. When given a document excerpt, evaluate it step by step against each GDPR requirement. For each step, quote the relevant text if found, or state it is missing. End with a structured verdict listing all violations. Always follow this exact format:

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): ...
Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): ...
Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): ...
Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): ...
Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): ...
Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): ...
Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): ...
Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): ...
Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): ...
Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): ...

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following mental health app privacy policy for GDPR compliance:

"MindSpace helps you track your mood, journal your thoughts, and access guided therapy sessions. We collect your journal entries, mood scores, therapy session notes, and device identifiers. We use this to personalise your experience and improve our algorithms. We may share insights with anonymised third-party research. Your data may be transferred to our servers in Canada. You can email us to delete your account."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [FAIL] No lawful basis stated for any processing activity.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [FAIL] Journal entries, mood scores, and therapy notes constitute health and mental health data — special category data under Art. 9. No explicit consent or Art. 9(2) basis is stated.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] Personalisation and algorithm improvement are stated. Research sharing is a secondary purpose requiring separate consent, especially given the sensitivity of mental health data.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] Device identifiers are collected but their necessity is not explained.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [FAIL] No retention period stated.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [FAIL] Only account deletion (erasure) is referenced via email. No other rights stated.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [FAIL] "Anonymised third-party research" is vague. Research partners are not named and no DPA is referenced.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [PARTIAL] Canada is disclosed as a transfer destination. Canada has an EU adequacy decision, but the adequacy basis is not cited.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [FAIL] Not addressed. Large-scale processing of mental health data almost certainly requires a DPO.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [FAIL] Not addressed.

VERDICT: Non-Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 6 (no lawful basis), Art. 9 (special category mental health data without valid basis), Art. 5 (no retention period), Art. 15-21 (rights absent), Art. 28 (research partners undisclosed), Art. 37 (DPO likely required), Art. 33 (no breach notification)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Obtain explicit consent separately for mental health data processing under Art. 9(2)(a)
- State lawful basis for each processing purpose
- Separate consent for research data sharing from core service consent
- Remove or justify collection of device identifiers
- State a retention period
- Add full data subject rights section
- Name research partners and confirm anonymisation standard
- Reference Canada adequacy decision as transfer basis
- Appoint and disclose a DPO
- Add breach notification commitment
